cases.title: A fact stated in session 1 is recalled in session 2

turns.prompt: kicking off my first week here

cases.note: The employer fact enters the store in session 1 (small talk, nothing to push yet) and must survive into a brand-new session 2 to answer the recall query. A session-scoped or window-limited memory that drops everything at session end fails this baseline.

cases.title: Habituation is per-session — a suppressed fact resurfaces in the next session

seed.statement: The user drinks an oat-milk latte.

cases.note: Turn 1 surfaces the preference; turn 2 (same session) is habituated away so the channel does not nag. Turn 3 opens a fresh session — habituation is session-scoped, so the fact is allowed to surface again. This is the behaviour the runner's session boundary enforces: crossing a session id resets per-session suppression while keeping the long-term fact.

cases.title: A role updated in a later session wins over the earlier value

seed.statement: The user is a QA engineer.
Update: the user moved to a backend engineer role.

cases.note: The role was captured in an early session and revised in a later one (the newer fact declares supersedes). By the time session 4 asks, only the current value may surface — the stale QA role must never resurface across the session gap. Same supersede semantics as STM-vs-LTM, but the correction lives in a different session than both the original and the query.

cases.title: Facts accumulated over several sessions are aggregated by a final query

seed.statement: The user is based in Kazan.
The user leads a small analytics team.
The user prefers PostgreSQL over MySQL.

turns.prompt: by the way I lead the analytics team
I prefer that database for everything
summarize everything you know about me

cases.note: Three facts land in three separate sessions, each recalled by its own cue as it is mentioned. A later session asks for a summary via a shared meta-cue ('about me') and must aggregate all three at once (within the delivery budget). Tests both cross-session persistence and multi-fact aggregation on one prompt.
